CDC Hepatitis B Basics

•

CDC Adult Immunization Schedule Notes

•

CDC MMWR: Universal Hepatitis B Vaccination in Adults 19–59, 2022

•

WHO Hepatitis B Fact Sheet

title: Where to Get a Yellow Fever Card in Lagos
seoTitle: Where to Get a Yellow Fever Card in Lagos
metaDescription: Find legit places to get a Yellow Fever card in Lagos, learn the 10-day rule, how to verify your card, and avoid fake certificates.
focusKeyword: Where to get a Yellow Fever Card in Lagos
html:
If you’re traveling from Lagos and your destination (or airline) asks for a Yellow Fever Card, what they really mean is the WHO International Certificate of Vaccination or Prophylaxis (ICVP)—the official yellow booklet used worldwide. The good news: you can get it in Lagos, but you want to do it the right way to avoid delays, denied boarding, or a card that gets rejected.

This guide covers legit places to get a Yellow Fever card in Lagos, the 10-day timing rule, and how to verify your card and avoid scams.

What a “Yellow Fever Card” really is ?

The “Yellow Fever card” is the International Certificate of Vaccination or Prophylaxis (ICVP). For it to count, it must be properly completed, signed, and stamped by the vaccination provider/center. The CDC notes the ICVP must be validated with the official stamp of the center where the vaccine was given, and travelers without a valid ICVP can face denied entry, quarantine, or revaccination at the border. (CDC – ICVP Guide)

The 10-day rule Lagos travelers often get wrong Your Yellow Fever certificate becomes valid 10 days after vaccination—not immediately. WHO states the Yellow Fever vaccination certificate is valid from 10 days after administration. (WHO – Yellow Fever Q&A)

WHO also updated international rules so the Yellow Fever certificate extends for the life of the person vaccinated, starting 10 days after vaccination. (WHO – Extension to Life Validity (PDF))

Why this matters in Lagos: a study conducted among Nigerian travelers at Murtala Muhammed International Airport (MMIA), Ikeja found that while 66.2% reported being vaccinated for Yellow Fever, 24.4% were vaccinated less than 10 days before travel—exactly the timing that can trigger travel-day problems.

Practical takeaway: If your flight is soon, don’t gamble—book your vaccination so your certificate is valid before departure.

Where to get a Yellow Fever card in Lagos 1) Port Health Services (official government route)

Nigeria’s NCDC states that international travelers can get vaccinated at least 10 days before departure at any Ports Health Service (PHS) office nationwide and obtain a valid vaccination card as evidence. (NCDC – Yellow Fever FAQs)

Port Health Services also describes its function as including immunization and issuing Yellow Cards. (Port Health Services Nigeria)

When Port Health is a good fit: 2) Inocul8 (clinic visits in Lagos)

If you prefer a clinic-based, guided experience (especially helpful if you want support with timing, documentation checks, and travel readiness), you can book Yellow Fever services through Inocul8. You get travel health consultation services regarding you trip.

Tip: No matter where you go, what matters is that you actually get vaccinated and your ICVP entry is correctly completed and validated.

Step-by-step: how to get your Yellow Fever card in Lagos 1) Plan around your travel date 2) Bring the right essentials 3) At the clinic: what should happen The provider completes your ICVP and validates it with a signature and official stamp of the vaccination center. CDC’s Yellow Book emphasizes that vaccinees should receive a completed ICVP signed and stamped, and that a properly completed ICVP is valid beginning 10 days after primary vaccination. (CDC – Yellow Book: Yellow Fever)

You receive the Yellow Fever vaccine.

How to verify your Yellow Fever card in Nigeria Nigeria’s Federal Ministry of Health FAQ explains you can verify your Yellow Card by using the official Yellow Card portal—either by entering your details or scanning the QR code on the card. (Nigeria FMoH – FAQs)

You can also access Nigeria’s portal here: (Yellow Card Nigeria Portal).

Note: These portals are no longer functional as a form of validation. The Nigerian government have informed other international bodies how to confirm authenticity of yellow cards from Nigeria.

Why verification matters A peer-reviewed paper on counterfeit vaccination certificates notes that counterfeit Yellow Fever certificates can seriously undermine disease control—and that Nigeria developed an “e-Yellow Card” verifiable by QR code because validating paper ICVPs can be difficult.

A simple “No-Scam” checklist (use this anywhere in Lagos)

Before you leave the provider, confirm your card has:

A travel-medicine paper summarizing ICVP requirements notes validity depends on documentation including date, clinician signature/status, manufacturing and batch number, and official stamp.

Quick FAQs Can I get the Yellow Fever card urgently?

You can often get vaccinated quickly, but the certificate becomes valid 10 days after vaccination—so “urgent” doesn’t change that rule. (CDC – ICVP Guide)

Does the Yellow Fever certificate expire?

Under WHO IHR updates, the Yellow Fever certificate is valid for life, starting 10 days after vaccination. (WHO – Extension to Life Validity (PDF))

(Older booklets may still show “10 years,” which is why travelers get confused.)

Can I travel if my vaccine is less than 10 days old?

That’s risky. CDC warns travelers without a valid ICVP may be denied entry, quarantined, or revaccinated at the point of entry. (CDC – ICVP Guide)

How do I verify a Nigerian Yellow Card?

Pending when it becomes functional, use the official portal/QR verification guidance from the Federal Ministry of Health. (Nigeria FMoH – FAQs). Best advise is for you to get the document from a reliable source.

Final takeaway If you’re traveling from Lagos, your safest plan is:

If you want a clinic-based option in Lagos with a smooth process and travel-medicine guidance, you can book via: (Inocul8 – Yellow Fever)

title: HPV Vaccine After 30: Too Late or Still a Power
seoTitle: HPV Vaccine After 30: Too Late or Still a Power
metaDescription: HPV vaccine after 30 in Lagos may still help. Learn who benefits most, adult dose schedule, side effects, and next steps.
html:
If you’re in Lagos and you care about your health, you’ve probably heard of HPV. Maybe you’ve also heard this rumor: “If you’re over 30, the HPV vaccine won’t help you.”

Here’s the friendly truth: for many adults, it’s not too late. Getting the HPV vaccine after 30 can still be a smart, protective move especially if your life could include new partners in the future.

Let’s break this down in a simple way.

What is HPV (and why should you care)?

HPV stands for human papillomavirus. It’s a very common virus. Most people, men and women will come across HPV at some point in their lives, often without even knowing it.

There are many types of HPV:

HPV can affect both men and women

HPV isn’t just a “women’s issue.” It can raise cancer risk in anyone. Over time, certain HPV types can lead to cancers like:

The hard part is this: HPV often has no symptoms at first. So someone can carry and pass it without knowing.

So what does the vaccine do?

The HPV vaccine is a prevention tool. Think of it like a strong umbrella:

That’s why people say it’s best to get it younger. But “best earlier” doesn’t mean “useless later.” Not at all.

HPV Vaccine After 30 — Why It Can Still Be a Power Move

So, is the HPV vaccine after 30 a waste? Nope. For many people, it can still be a smart, protective choice.

Here’s the simplest way to think about it:

The vaccine protects you from future HPV types you haven’t met yet

The HPV vaccine is like a security guard. It helps stop new HPV infections from certain HPV types that are known to cause cancer and genital warts. But it doesn’t erase HPV you already have.

That means the vaccine can still help if:

And let’s be real—life can change. People move, date, marry, divorce, remarry, or have new partners. So even if you’re over 30, you may still want that extra layer of protection.

What do medical experts say about adults 27–45?

Health experts don’t say, “Everyone over 30 must get it.” Instead, they recommend something called shared clinical decision-making for adults ages 27 to 45.

That’s a fancy way of saying:

This guidance exists because:

So it’s not a “too late” situation for HPV vaccine after 30. It’s more like:

“Let’s make a smart choice based on your life.”

Who is most likely to benefit after 30?

You may be more likely to benefit if any of these feel true:

  • If you’re below 21 and taking the vaccine for the first time
  • Above 21 and not having HPV diseases
  • If you have been exposed to certain HPV strains but not most strains the vaccine prevents

Is the HPV vaccine safe after 30?

Yes. The HPV vaccines have been studied for years, and safety tracking has included millions of doses worldwide. For adults ages 27–45, HPV vaccine after 30 studies show the vaccine is generally well tolerated, and the immune system still responds well.

In plain words: most people do fine.

Common side effects (usually mild)

Most side effects are short-lived and go away on their own. You might notice:

These are signs your immune system is practicing how to protect you.

Less common reactions

Less common issues can happen with any vaccine, like:

If you’ve had a serious allergic reaction to a vaccine ingredient before, that’s something to bring up with a healthcare professional before you get the shot.

How many doses do adults need?

For adults who start the HPV vaccine at age 21 or older—including those in their 30s and 40s the usual schedule is:

Two doses.

Here’s the simple timeline:

  • Start dose (1st shot): the day of your visit to the clinic
  • Second dose (2nd shot): six months from the day you started

That’s it. Two steps. Done.

Why two doses for adults?

  • Younger teens (9 to 20 years) most times need only one dose because their immune systems respond very strongly at that age.
  • Adults need two (21 years and above) doses to build the best and longest-lasting protection.

Think of it like building a strong wall:

What if you miss a dose?

Life happens. Lagos traffic alone can delay anybody!

If you miss a dose:

A healthcare provider can guide you on the correct timing.

Does the protection last?

Studies show that the HPV vaccine provides long-lasting protection. Research has followed vaccinated people for many years and found strong immune responses over time.

Scientists continue to monitor protection, and so far, the results are reassuring.

HPV Vaccine + Screening — Why You Still Need Check-Ups

Here’s a super important point (and many people miss it):

Even if you get the HPV vaccine, screening still matters

The HPV vaccine helps protect you from new infections from certain HPV types. But it does not:

So, vaccination and screening work like a team.

For women: cervical cancer screening is still key

If you have a cervix, screening can find changes early—before they turn into cancer. In simple terms:

Your healthcare professional can guide you on what tests you need and how often (this can depend on age and past results).

For men and women: don’t ignore symptoms

Most HPV infections cause no symptoms, but it’s still smart to see a clinician if you notice:

No panic—just don’t “manage it at home” for months. Early checks are a power move too.

The “power move” health plan (simple version)

If you’re 30+ and thinking about the HPV vaccine, a strong plan often looks like this:

Who Should Seriously Consider the HPV Vaccine After 30?

Let’s make this super practical. If you’re 30+ in Lagos, HPV vaccine after 30 may still be a strong health move—especially if your future could include new exposure to HPV.

A quick checklist: You may benefit more if…

You should seriously consider the vaccine if any of these fit you:

This fits with the CDC’s advice that adults ages 27–45 may choose vaccination based on shared clinical decision-making—meaning you and your healthcare professional decide together based on your life and health goals.

Friendly real talk

Getting the HPV vaccine after 30 isn’t about fear. It’s about planning—like wearing a seatbelt even if you’re a careful driver. If your life could include new risk later, the vaccine can be a smart layer of protection.

What to Do Next

title: How to Get Yellow Fever Card in Nigeria: A Simple Guide
seoTitle: How to Get Yellow Fever Card in Nigeria: A Simple Guide
metaDescription: Planning an international trip? Learn how to get a yellow fever card in Nigeria. This simple guide provide the cost and location.
focusKeyword: how to get yellow fever card in nigeria
html:
Are you planning to travel outside of Nigeria? If you are between 21 and 55 years old, booking a plane ticket is just the start. You also need an E-Yellow Card.

It is very important to know how to get a yellow fever card in Nigeria. If you don't have one, the airport might not let you fly. This digital card proves you had a shot to protect you from the yellow fever virus. International rules say you must have this card to visit many countries that the disease rarely occurs.

Why Do You Need This Card?

The yellow fever card for travel is more than just a piece of paper. It helps keep everyone healthy. Many countries in Africa and South America ask for this proof to stop diseases from spreading.

Nigeria does not use the old paper cards anymore. Now, everyone uses the "E-Yellow Card." This new card has a special QR code on it. Health officers scan this code to make sure your shot is real. Without this card, you might not get a visa or be allowed on your flight.

How Much Does it Cost?

A common question is: how much is a yellow card?

•

The Price: The fee for the card is N5,000.

•

How to Pay: You must pay online through a website called Remita on the Port Health Services portal.

•

Important Note: This money only pays for the card. If you have not had the actual shot yet, you must go to a health center to get it.

Finding a Place to Get the Shot

You must get the vaccine before you can apply for the card. If you are looking for a yellow fever vaccine near me, check these places:

•

Clinics or Health centers approved by the government.

•

Port Health offices at big airports.

•

Special offices at seaports.

The best part is that one shot usually protects you for your whole life! However, you might still need to update your digital card on the website every few years.

Getting Your Card in Lagos

Lagos is the busiest place for travel in Nigeria. Getting a yellow fever card in Lagos is easy if you follow these steps:

•

Pay Online: Go to the official website and pay the fee.

•

Go to the Airport: Visit the Port Health office at the Murtala Muhammed International Airport.

•

Show Proof: Bring your vaccination record and your payment receipt.

•

Get Your Card: They will take your information and print your new card.

Pro Tip: Do this at least 10 days before you travel. It takes that long for the vaccine to start working in your body.

Need Extra Help?

Using government websites can be confusing and slow. If you want to make sure your papers are ready on time, it helps to ask for help.

If you need help with your certificates, shots or travel rules, talk to the experts at www.inocul8.com.ng. or click the WhatsApp icon below. We make the health steps easy so you can enjoy your trip!

title: Is the HPV Vaccine Safe? The Truth About Side Effects
seoTitle: Is the HPV Vaccine Safe? The Truth About Side Effects
metaDescription: Is the HPV vaccine safe? We break down common HPV vaccine side effects like sore arms and dizziness while debunking myths about severe risks.
html:
[caption id="attachment_2886" align="aligncenter" width="300"] HPV vaccine side effects like a sore arm are usually mild and temporary, making the shot a safe choice for cancer prevention.[/caption]

Is the HPV Vaccine Safe? Everything you should know

If you are thinking about getting the HPV vaccine (or getting it for your child), it is normal to have questions. You might feel concerned: "Will it hurt?" or "Are there any bad reactions?"

The short answer is: Yes, the vaccine is very safe.

Scientists and doctors have studied millions of doses given all over the world, and the results are clear: the protection against cancer is massive, and the side effects are mild and temporary.

Here is a simple breakdown of what to expect, based on real medical evidence.

1. The Most Common Reaction: "The Sore Arm"

The most common side effect is exactly what you expect from any shot—your arm might feel a little sore.

According to a major study of the HPV vaccine, the most frequently reported issues were non-serious. You might notice:

•

Pain, redness, or swelling right where the needle went in.

•

A mild headache or feeling a bit tired for a day.

Think of it like the soreness you get after a tetanus shot. It usually goes away on its own within a day or two.

2. Dizziness and Fainting (It’s Not Just the Vaccine!)

Some teenagers feel dizzy or even faint after getting the shot. Doctors call this "syncope".

Here is the interesting part: studies show this is often caused by anxiety or stress about the needle itself, rather than the ingredients in the vaccine. It is a common reaction when adolescents get any kind of injection.

The easy fix: To prevent dizziness, doctors recommend sitting or lying down for about 15 minutes after getting the shot. That’s it!

3. What About Serious Side Effects?

You may have seen scary stories online about "severe" reactions. They're mostly promoted by groups against vaccines called "anti-vaxxers". Let’s look at the facts.

•

Allergic Reactions: Serious allergic reactions (anaphylaxis) are extremely rare. One large study found this happens in only about 1.7 cases for every million doses given. This is why nurses ask you to wait for 15 minutes, so they can help immediately if you have an allergy.

•

Autoimmune Diseases: Some people worry that the vaccine could confuse the immune system. However, a massive review in 2025 looked at over 8 million people and found no increased risk of autoimmune diseases like diabetes, thyroid issues, or lupus after HPV vaccination.

•

Other Conditions: Extensive research involving millions of doses has checked for conditions like Guillain-Barré Syndrome (muscle weakness) or POTS (heart rate issues). The World Health Organization (WHO) and other researchers have confirmed there is no evidence linking the vaccine to these problems.

The Bottom Line

No medical treatment is 100% free of side effects, but for the HPV vaccine, the risks are tiny compared to the benefits.

The "price" you pay is usually just a sore arm or a brief moment of dizziness. In exchange, you get powerful protection against cervical cancer and other dangerous diseases.

To Learn more

You can book a consultation with the Inocul8 doctor's or call/WhatsApp 07063736485.

References & Further Reading

•

Pediatrics (2019): Safety of the 9-Valent Human Papillomavirus Vaccine.

•

Vaccines (2025): No Increased Risk of Autoimmune Diseases Following HPV Vaccination.

•

World Health Organization (2017): Global Advisory Committee on Vaccine Safety.

•

Vaccine (2011): Monitoring the safety of quadrivalent human papillomavirus vaccine.

title: HPV Treatment Cost in Nigeria? A Cost Breakdown
seoTitle: HPV Treatment Cost in Nigeria? A Cost Breakdown
metaDescription: HPV Treatment Cost in Nigeria? We break down the costs of treating genital warts vs. the price of the HPV vaccine.
html:
[caption id="attachment_2880" align="aligncenter" width="300"] A visual breakdown showing the stark contrast between the unpredictable, high HPV treatment cost in Nigeria and the fixed, affordable investment of vaccination.[/caption]

HPV: What's The True Cost of Prevention vs. Treatment in Nigeria

When it comes to your health and your wallet, one critical question often arises: Is it cheaper to treat a disease or to prevent it? Regarding the Human Papillomavirus (HPV), the answer is financially and medically clear. Prevention through the HPV vaccine costs significantly less than the burden of treatment.

HPV is not just a small infection; it is a leading cause of genital warts and serious conditions like cancers. Once an HPV-related disease develops, the road to recovery can be long, emotionally stressful, discomforting, and incredibly expensive.

In this article, we determined the real costs of HPV treatment versus vaccination in Nigeria.

HPV Treatment Cost in Nigeria: A Breakdown of Warts & Cancer Fees

Many people ignore the financial implications of HPV infections until the signs and symptoms begin. If you develop genital warts, the treatment costs rise quickly.

1. Creams and Medications

For the initial treatment of genital warts, doctors often prescribe topical creams. However, these are not one-time costs.

•

Podophyllin cream: This typically costs between ₦3,000 and ₦10,000 per pack. Unfortunately, a single bottle is rarely enough to manage the warts. It doesn't completely clear the bumps like tissues. And if you buy the paint dosage form or use it inappropriately, it burns your genitals.

  • Imiquimod cream: This medication, which helps boost the body’s immune response, is more effective but comes with a higher price tag. A treatment course typically costs between ₦15,000 and ₦50,000.

2. Medical Procedures

If creams fail to remove the warts, patients are often referred for physical procedures. These methods are invasive and require repeated hospital visits.

  • Cryotherapy: This involves freezing the warts off. The cost ranges from approximately ₦10,000 to ₦25,000 per session. Since multiple sessions are often required, the total bill can easily double or triple.
  • Laser Therapy: For stubborn cases, laser therapy can effectively burn off infected tissue. This procedure is significantly more expensive, costing ₦50,000 to ₦100,000 or more, depending on the facility.
  • Immune Therapy: This trains the immune system to prevent the recurrence of warts. Restoring normal life. It costs about #250,000 to #350,000, involving multiple visits. It's the most effective and gives sustained results.

3. The Cost of Cancer Care

The most severe consequence of untreated HPV is cancer, particularly cervical and throat and neck cancer. The financial burden here is devastating.

•

Comprehensive Care: Between immunotherapy, surgery, chemotherapy, and frequent hospital admissions, care can cost hundreds of thousands to millions of naira.

•

Long-term Impact: For many Nigerian families, these costs are unsustainable, creating a significant debt burden.

Why HPV Vaccination is the Smarter Choice

Now, let’s compare those unpredictable treatment costs to the fixed, one-time cost of prevention.

The HPV vaccine price in Nigeria generally ranges from ₦60,000 to ₦120,000 per dose. Since most adults require two doses for full protection, the total investment is roughly ₦100,000 to ₦220,000. This depends on the vaccine brand and the immunization centre offering the service.

HPV Treatment Cost in Nigeria vs. Vaccine Price: The Financial Verdict

For a maximum of roughly ₦120,000, you gain years of protection against the most dangerous strains of HPV.

•

Vaccination: ~₦200,000 (Total for full protection).

•

Treatment: Can range from ₦50,000 for minor procedures to millions of naira for cancer care.

Research from health economics studies in Nigeria confirms that HPV vaccination is not just safer—it is far more cost-effective. By choosing the vaccine, you avoid the recurring costs of creams, the pain of procedures, and the massive expense of cancer treatment.

Protect Your Health and Your Finances Today

Choosing HPV vaccination is an investment in a healthier, debt-free future. It protects you and your family from unnecessary medical bills and severe health risks.

Prevention is smarter. Prevention is cheaper. And it starts with the HPV vaccine.

title: HPV Vaccine Dose: Why the Dose Schedule Changed (Part 2)
seoTitle: HPV Vaccine Dose: Why the Dose Schedule Changed (Part 2)
metaDescription: HPV vaccine dose. Why has the HPV vaccine schedule changed? Discover the updated WHO guidelines for single and two-dose schedules.
html:
[caption id="attachment_2849" align="aligncenter" width="300"] Recent updates from the WHO suggest fewer HPV vaccine doses are needed to provide robust protection against cervical cancer.[/caption]

Why the Dose Schedule Was Modified (Part 2)

HPV Vaccine Dose Predicting Success with Computers

As regards the HPV vaccine dose update. To understand what might happen in the future, scientists used advanced computer programs to predict health trends in the U.S. over the next 100 years. They looked at the costs and benefits of different vaccination schedules.

The computer models showed that giving two doses of the HPV vaccine is a smart way to save money. In fact, two doses prevent cancer almost as well as three doses. However, the researchers noted a catch: this strategy works best if the protection from those two shots stays strong in the body for at least 20 years.

HPV Vaccine Dose Real-World Proof

Scientists backed up these computer predictions by comparing results from two major studies in Tanzania and Kenya involving more than 1,200 participants.

They discovered that giving just a single shot of the vaccine to girls aged 9 to 14 created a strong immune defense. Two years later, their bodies were just as ready to fight the virus as the bodies of young women aged 15 to 20. Since we already know that one shot effectively stops infections in the older group , this comparison confirms that a single shot should protect younger girls just as well.

Conclusion: The New Rules

Thanks to this strong evidence, the World Health Organization (WHO) has updated its guidance. Girls and young women aged 9 to 20 can now receive just one or two doses of the HPV vaccine. For women older than 21, the recommendation is to receive two doses spaced six months apart. However, individuals with weakened immune systems, such as those living with HIV, still need three doses to ensure full protection. You can read part 1 of the article here.

title: HPV Vaccine Dose: Why Fewer Doses Are Now Recommended
seoTitle: HPV Vaccine Dose: Why Fewer Doses Are Now Recommended
metaDescription: Discover why the WHO updated HPV vaccine dose guidelines. New research proves fewer doses provide robust immune defense.
html:
[caption id="attachment_2849" align="aligncenter" width="300"] Recent updates from the WHO suggest fewer HPV vaccine doses are needed to provide robust protection against cervical cancer.[/caption] HPV Vaccine Update: Why the Dose Schedule Was Modified (Part 1)

The HPV vaccine is a powerful tool used to prevent diseases, particularly cervical cancer, caused by the human papillomavirus. When the vaccines were first approved for public use between 2006 and 2014, the standard hpv vaccine dose schedule was rigorous. Generally, children aged 9 to 14 years received two doses, while individuals aged 15 and above were required to take three doses.

However, in 2022, the World Health Organization (WHO) updated these recommendations, suggesting fewer doses for specific age groups. Why was this change made? Extensive global research has revealed that fewer doses can be just as effective. Here is the science behind the new HPV vaccine guidelines.

HPV vaccine dose: Strong Immune Defense in Younger Girls

One of the key reasons for the change came from a major research in Canada. Scientists discovered that girls aged 9 to 13 who received just two doses of the HPV vaccine developed an immune defense that was non-inferior to—and in some cases better than—young women aged 16 to 26 who received the standard three doses.

In fact, the study showed that the younger girls produced significantly higher levels of virus-fighting antibodies after two doses compared to the older group after three. This suggested early on that younger immune systems respond very robustly to the vaccine.

Evidence from Large International Trials

Further evidence emerged by combining results from a large international trial (PATRICIA) and a specific vaccine trial in Costa Rica. Researchers tracked over 26,000 women for four years to see how well the vaccine worked in real-world scenarios.

The findings were groundbreaking: receiving just one or two shots of the HPV vaccine was as effective at protecting against the main cancer-causing virus types (HPV 16 and 18) as the standard three-shot schedule. This indicated that a reduced schedule could still offer high levels of protection.

The Power of a Single Dose

More recent studies have focused specifically on the efficacy of a single hpv vaccine dose. A pivotal study involving over 2,000 young women in Kenya found that receiving just a single shot of the HPV vaccine is incredibly effective.

Over the course of 18 months, the researchers found that one dose stopped 97.5% of dangerous, cancer-causing virus infections. This demonstrated that a single dose provides powerful protection, comparable to multidose regimens, for those who might otherwise go unvaccinated.

title: HPV Vaccine: Do They Prevent Cancers?

html:
[caption id="attachment_2826" align="aligncenter" width="300"] HPV: Protecting yourself is a powerful step toward a cancer-free future.[/caption]

HPV Vaccines: Do the Vaccines Prevent Cancers?

Cancer occurs when abnormal cells in the body grow out of control and spread, causing severe health problems. The Human Papillomavirus (HPV)is a group of viruses linked to the development of several types of cancer.

While vaccines are available to fight this virus, many people ask: Do these vaccines really help prevent cancer? Here is what major scientific research has found regarding throat, anal, and cervical cancers.

1. HPV Vaccine: Throat and Neck Cancer

Researchers in Costa Rica conducted a study involving 7,466 women aged 18 to 25. Their goal was to see if the HPV vaccine could prevent infections in the mouth that can lead to throat and neck cancer. The study found that the vaccine was 93.3% effective at stopping these specific oral infections. This is significant news because it suggests that the vaccine provides strong protection against oral HPV infections, potentially preventing future oral cancers.

2. HPV Vaccine: Anal Cancer

Between 2004 and 2008, researchers studied 602 men to determine if the vaccine prevents anal cancer caused by the HPV virus. To ensure the results were applicable to different people, the research was conducted in seven countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, Croatia, Germany, Spain, and the United States. Men who received the vaccine had significantly fewer dangerous infections and precancerous spots compared to those who did not. These results prove that the HPV vaccine is a safe and powerful tool for reducing the risk of anal cancer.

3. HPV Vaccine: Cervical Cancer

A major international study wanted to find out if the vaccine could stop HPV from causing serious changes in the cervix that lead to cancer. This massive trial included 12,167 women aged 15 to 26. The vaccine worked extremely well. It prevented 98% of serious cervical problems (high-grade lesions) caused by HPV types 16 and 18 in women. This confirms that getting the HPV vaccine significantly reduces the chance of developing conditions that lead to cervical cancer.

title: Hepatitis B: Cost to treat in Nigeria?
seoTitle: Hepatitis B: Cost to treat in Nigeria?
metaDescription: Hepatitis B is a silent illness with a huge financial impact. We reveal the true annual cost of managing Hep B in Nigeria.
html:
[caption id="attachment_2809" align="aligncenter" width="300"] Regular specialist consultations and hospital visits are a critical, yet expensive, part of long-term Hepatitis B management in Nigeria.[/caption]

Hepatitis B: How Much Does it Cost to Manage in Nigeria?

Imagine an enemy that you can live with in the same house for years without knowing. This is the situation of people living with hepatitis B infection. A liver disease caused by the hepatitis B virus (HBV). The illness can be prevented by taking three doses of the hepatitis vaccine, also known as hepb vaccine. Most patients ask, "hepatitis b is it curable?'' Presently, there is no cure, so infected individuals will need to manage their health for an extended period. About 20 million Nigerians are living with the virus. Nine out of 10 don’t know they have it. These patients will need to manage their health through regular check-ups, a balanced diet, and the use of medications. This article presents the financial implications of hepatitis B treatment in Nigeria, drawing on research findings done in the country.

Cost of hepatitis B management in Nigeria

The National Financial Burden

According to Dr. Godwin Ntadom, Director of the Public Health Department at the Federal Ministry of Health (FMOH), the country's collective expenses on hepatitis range between 13.3 and 17.9 trillion Naira. But the question the average Nigerian asks is: What does it cost me to manage a long-term HBV infection?

The Patient’s Burden of Hepatitis B

To understand the financial weight of this disease, we have categorised the costs into direct medical, direct non-medical, and indirect costs based on recent research estimates.

  • Direct Medical Costs (#1, 413, 090 or $933/year)

These are the expenses strictly related to clinical care. For a person living with HBV, this includes:

  • Hospital registration and doctor consultation fees.
  • Antiviral and liver-protective medications.
  • Crucial laboratory tests such as Hepatitis B DNA viral load, serology, liver function tests, and abdominal ultrasound scans.
  • Direct Non-Medical Costs (# 202, 215 or $133/year)

Treatment doesn't happen in a vacuum. These are the "hidden" out-of-pocket expenses, including transportation to clinics, meals during hospital visits, and the costs incurred by caregivers or family members accompanying the patient.

  • Indirect Healthcare Costs (#640,095 or $421/year)

This represents the "opportunity cost" of the disease. It quantifies the economic loss when a patient is unable to work or spend time productively due to illness or fatigue.

With an estimated total of $ 2,246,978 or $1,487 per year, managing Hepatitis B is a significant financial burden. For many Nigerian families, this amount is high enough to drive them into poverty as they battle to save the life of a loved one.

Conclusion

Managing this liver condition in Nigeria is undeniably a costly endeavour. It requires a significant out-of-pocket expenditure that most individuals cannot sustain over the long term.

The most financially sound strategy is prevention using solutions like hepatitis b vaccination or hep b vaccination. It saves both money and lives. To understand how to protect yourself and your family from this financial and physical burden, you can consult with the doctors at Inocul8 today.

title: HPV Vaccine: Does It Cause Infertility?
seoTitle: HPV Vaccine: Does It Cause Infertility?
metaDescription: Worried the HPV vaccine might cause infertility? Learn how large studies show it’s safe and does not harm your child’s future fertility.
html:
HPV Vaccine: What Effect Does It have on fertility?

James was worried about taking his daughters for the human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccination after hearing the vaccine caused infertility.

The human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine received authorisation for public use in 2006 to prevent diseases and cancers caused by the human papillomavirus.  Parents are concerned that vaccinating their children against the human papillomavirus (HPV) might affect their ability to give birth later. Does the HPV vaccine cause infertility?

The HPV vaccine and its effect on fertility

Infertility is the inability of a couple to conceive after a year of regular and unprotected sexual intercourse.

According to the World Health Organisation (WHO), primary infertility is a situation where a woman has never gotten pregnant. Secondary infertility is when a woman has given birth earlier but now experiences difficulty in getting pregnant.

In the US, research conducted among women aged 18 to 33 found no link between the HPV vaccine and infertility. The scientists concluded that there was no evidence of a rise in infertility cases in women who received the HPV vaccine.

Similarly, a study was done to assess the effect of HPV immunisation on how easily women get pregnant. The experiment involved 3,483 women and 1,022 men participants. The researchers found that among women who had past sexually transmitted infections (STIs), the vaccine made it easy for them to become pregnant.  A nationwide study was done in Denmark among 996,300 girls and women aged 11 to 34 years from 2007 to 2016. The researchers concluded that HPV vaccination is safe and doesn’t affect the ovaries of women nor their ability to conceive.  In conclusion, if you’re a parent worried about the possible effect of the HPV vaccination on the fertility of your child. Don't be troubled; scientific findings have shown that the vaccine has no negative effect on the fertility of those who received it.  To learn more about the HPV vaccination, you can consult Inocul8 today and have our doctors provide you with detailed information.

Preventing HBV in Lagos: Inocul8’s 2019 World Hepatitis Day Outreach

Inocul8’s mission is to help people and families prevent infectious diseases, as we believe no one should suffer or die from illnesses that existing solutions can prevent.

In 2019, to celebrate World Hepatitis Day (observed annually on July 28), we conducted a successful outreach program in Iyana Paja, Lagos State. The initiative focused on education, screening, and prevention.

Educating the Community

The outreach began with an educational session. Participants learned that hepatitis B is a liver disease caused by the Hepatitis B virus (HBV). They were strongly encouraged to participate in the screening exercise to determine their current status. We emphasised that individuals who tested negative should pursue vaccination.

Screening Participants for Hepatitis B

The Inocul8 team provided HBV screening to all participants, enabling them to know their infection status. The screening test works by detecting Hepatitis B surface proteins (HBsAg), which are present in anyone with the virus. The rapid test involves collecting a small blood sample and analysing it for these specific viral proteins.

Vaccination for Prevention

The final activity was offering vaccination to participants who tested negative for hepatitis B. The goal of the vaccination was to provide long-term protection against the infection in case of future exposure. Individuals require three doses of the vaccine to be fully immunised.

title: Meet the Founders
seoTitle: Meet the Founders
html:
Meet the Founders

A long time ago, long before Inocul8 had a logo, a clinic, or even a name—a seed was planted.

Farmers say the best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago; the next best time is now. In many ways, that is how the Inocul8 story began: as a quiet seed of an idea that the future of health in Africa should be built on prevention, not on pain.

Inocul8 was officially founded in 2019 by Dr Osezua Momoh and Dr Emmanuel Egbroko, with a clear mission: to help families prevent communicable diseases. The spark for this mission, however, started years earlier. With a near-death experience Emmanuel had while serving as a Youth Corps member in Northern Nigeria.

Before Inocul8, they were just two young doctors in their twenties, full of questions and restless energy.

In 2016, Emmanuel and Momoh met at a Young African Leaders Initiative (YALI) Regional Leadership Center program at GIMPA in Accra, Ghana. Surrounded by other young leaders dreaming about the future of the continent, they began asking themselves a hard question:

“If we really want to change health in our country, where do we start?”

They both knew the truth: health insurance coverage was low, hospital wards were full, and people were still dying from diseases that had vaccines and simple preventive solutions. The more they spoke, the clearer it became—prevention wasn’t just important; it was the only logical starting point. Focusing on preventive healthcare, they decided, would be the best thing after sliced bread.

After YALI, life took them to different corners of Nigeria, but their shared mission followed them.

In December 2016, Momoh went to serve at Lagos State University Teaching Hospital (LASUTH), where he helped manage people living with HIV.

Emmanuel served at the General Hospital in Minna, Niger State, where he provided access to medications for patients living with HIV.

There, Emmanuel witnessed the reality of preventable disease up close:

  • He saw a high burden of hepatitis B among patients visiting the hospital.
  • He saw children on the streets who had lost their limbs to poliovirus infection.
  • He experienced communities struggling with poor access to clean water.

He carried these images with him and often called Momoh to share what he was seeing. Those conversations slowly turned into conviction: this was not how things had to be.

Then came the moment that changed everything.

In April 2017, Emmanuel fell critically ill and was hospitalised at the General Hospital in Minna with severe typhoid fever. Lying on the hospital bed, weak and unsure of what would happen next, he experienced firsthand what it meant to be on the other side of care—afraid, vulnerable, and dependent on a system already under pressure.

That hospital stay became his turning point.

It transformed their shared ideas from “someone should do something” into “we must build something.” That “something” eventually became Inocul8.

In Emmanuel’s words:

"We started Inocul8 to help people prevent infectious diseases because we didn’t want people to die from diseases that have affordable preventive solutions. I didn’t want people to experience what I went through in the hospital. I wanted access to prevention to be easy and convenient for everyone."

From two young doctors with a shared conviction to a mission-driven brand, Inocul8 exists today because of one simple belief:

No one should suffer or die from a disease we already know how to prevent.

title: HPV Vaccine After 25: What Every Adult Should Know Part 1
seoTitle: HPV Vaccine After 25: What Every Adult Should Know Part 1
metaDescription: Can adults 26 and above get the HPV vaccine? Discover how HPV vaccination protects women at any age from cervical cancer and genital warts.
html:
[caption id="attachment_2763" align="aligncenter" width="300"] A confident woman stands as a reminder that HPV vaccination empowers adults to protect their health at any age.[/caption]

Funke was scrolling through posts on Facebook when she encountered content from a popular healthcare influencer on the human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine and how it helps girls aged 9 to 14 prevent genital warts and cervical cancer.

“What about me?” she wondered. “I’m over 25 — can’t it help me avoid the pain and suffering from HPV-related diseases?”

Like Funke, you might be asking a similar question: Can adults over 25 get the HPV vaccine? This piece provides the burning answers you’ve been looking for.

What Are the Chances of Getting HPV After 25?

The risk of getting HPV after age 25 remains significant. The infection is most common during youth, especially because of social behaviours and risky lifestyles. In fact, within four years of becoming sexually active, about 63% of women become infected.

But the risk doesn’t end there — people over 25 can still catch HPV and remain at risk for the rest of their lives. Once infected, the virus might disappear or stay hidden in the body, only to come back later.

Unlike viruses such as chickenpox, getting HPV once doesn’t protect you from future infections. You can get infected again with the same or different HPV types, even if your body cleared the first infection.

Your chance of HPV infection after 25 increases with new sexual partners. This shows that natural protection doesn’t do much to defend against the virus throughout a woman’s life. In fact, many studies have shown a second rise in HPV cases after age 45 or 50, often around the time of menopause.

Why HPV Vaccination Matters for Adults

Over the past 30 years, people have been getting married later and more have experienced divorce. Because of this, it has become more common for people to have new romantic or sexual partners both before marriage and again in middle age, which adds to the ongoing risk of HPV.

If both young adults and older women still face this risk, why aren’t more adults being vaccinated? Some healthcare providers believe that older women must have already been infected, so the HPV vaccine for adults wouldn’t be useful. But is there a way to check if someone had HPV before getting vaccinated?

Currently, no commercial tests are available to confirm past HPV infections before vaccination. The tests that would be needed aren’t widely accessible, and even if they were, they would likely be expensive and difficult to use in many parts of the world — especially in regions like Nigeria and other low- and middle-income countries.

That’s why HPV vaccination for women over 25 remains one of the most practical and powerful ways to protect against cervical cancer, genital warts, and other HPV-related diseases.

In part two, we discussed what happened when the vaccine was administered to people over 25 years, and the result will amaze you. To learn more about HPV and how it can be prevented, you can follow us on Instagram or sign up for our newsletter.

title: Why Yellow Fever Vaccination is a Mandatory Travel Requirement for Nigerians
seoTitle: Why Yellow Fever Vaccination is a Mandatory Travel Requirement for Nigerians
metaDescription: Learn why yellow fever vaccination is a mandatory travel requirement for Nigerians. Understand the WHO law, the yellow card certificate process, and how to stay compliant before your next trip.
html:
✈️ A Story Many Travellers Can Relate To

Dayo, a 34-year-old Nigerian, was preparing for his long-awaited trip to Brazil when his travel agent called with bad news: his documents were incomplete because his yellow card certificate was missing.

He was shocked and frustrated. “I’ve submitted every document! Why is this yellow card such a big deal?” he asked.

That simple question opens up an important conversation: Why is yellow fever vaccination mandatory for Nigerians travelling to certain countries?

What is Yellow Fever?

Yellow fever is a viral hemorrhagic disease (similar to Ebola) transmitted by infected Aedes aegypti mosquitoes.

Nigeria is one of several countries where yellow fever outbreaks occur regularly, and the disease remains a major public health concern.

Travellers from Nigeria who plan to visit countries where yellow fever is not endemic must show proof of vaccination through a yellow card certificate.

Yet, many Nigerians still don’t realise that this small yellow booklet is a crucial travel document, as essential as a passport or visa.

Why Yellow Fever Vaccination is Required for Nigerians

Yellow fever is endemic in Nigeria, meaning the virus circulates continuously in the environment. Occasional outbreaks lead to nationwide vaccination campaigns to control transmission.

When an unvaccinated traveller from Nigeria visits a country where yellow fever isn’t common, there’s a risk of importing the virus into that country.

This could expose people who have no immunity and cause new outbreaks in previously unaffected areas.

There’s also no specific treatment for yellow fever, only supportive care.

This makes prevention through vaccination the best and only effective protection.

The Legal Side: International Health Regulations (IHR 2005)

Some travellers joke, “I don’t need the shot, just give me the certificate.”

But here’s the truth: this isn’t optional, it’s international law.

Under the World Health Organisation’s International Health Regulations (IHR 2005), yellow fever is the only disease that legally requires proof of vaccination for international travel.

Countries have the authority to:

•

Deny entry to unvaccinated travellers from risk zones (like Nigeria).

•

Request valid yellow cards at airports and border posts.

•

Quarantine travellers who fail to comply.

Your yellow card certificate is officially known as the International Certificate of Vaccination or Prophylaxis (ICVP). Immigration officers will check it upon arrival and verify its authenticity.

Trying to bypass the system with fake documents isn’t just risky — it’s a violation of international law.

Why It’s Especially Important for Nigerians

Nigeria has recorded recurrent yellow fever outbreaks in states such as Bauchi, Delta, Benue, and Edo. Because of this, the country is classified by the WHO as high-risk for yellow fever transmission.

As a result, many countries, especially in South America, Asia, and the Middle East, require Nigerian travellers to show proof of vaccination before entry or even during layovers.

This isn’t just about bureaucracy; it’s a global safety measure designed to protect other populations from imported infections.

⚠️ What Happens If You’re Not Vaccinated

Skipping your yellow fever vaccination can lead to serious consequences:

•

❌ You may be denied boarding at the airport.

•

Immigration may refuse your entry upon arrival.

•

You could be quarantined at your own expense.

•

You might miss business meetings or conferences after paying for tickets and hotels.

•

And if you fall ill abroad, medical treatment can be costly — especially without travel insurance.

How Long Does the Vaccine Last?

Good news! The yellow fever vaccine is highly effective and provides lifelong immunity after a single dose.

It becomes valid 10 days after vaccination, and the certificate remains valid for life under the WHO regulations.

Ensure you receive the vaccination at an accredited facility in Nigeria to obtain a legitimate yellow card. To learn more, please contact Inocul8 on +2347063736482 or visit our website and Google page.

title: Why do Hospitals make it mandatory for Pregnant women to screen for HIV and Hepatitis?
seoTitle: Why do Hospitals make it mandatory for Pregnant women to screen for HIV and Hepatitis?
html:
Protect your little one with Hepatitis Screening During Pregnancy Pregnancy is a beautiful journey filled with anticipation, dreams, and heavy responsibility. While many women focus on eating right, taking vitamins, and attending routine checkups, one important health check often goes unnoticed—hepatitis screening.

My Story In 2021, I was posted to a general hospital in a South-South state of Nigeria for my National Youth Service Corps (NYSC). Honestly, I wasn’t thrilled. I had hoped for a big city experience—Abuja, Lagos, or even Port Harcourt. I tried every connection I had, but nothing worked. Eventually, I packed my bags and made the long, bumpy journey to my new station. As I watched the endless stretch of bushes pass by, I cried. This wasn’t the NYSC I imagined, but everything changed when I arrived at the hospital.  Next to the main hospital stood a beautifully structured building with the sign: “Maternity Referral Centre.” That building gave me hope—it stood as a testament to something good happening in that community. I soon learned from one of the nurses that the state government had built the Centre to reduce the high maternal death rate in the area. Most women in the community gave birth at home or with traditional birth attendants, without proper medical supervision. Sadly, complications were common. Many of these complications led to death.

Over time, some of these mothers and their babies would come to the hospital with unusual symptoms such as yellowing of the skin and eyes (jaundice). Most tests revealed that both mother and child were hepatitis positive. Shocked, many mothers would ask, “How could my baby have hepatitis?”

That question sparked a wave of awareness in the community about the importance of antenatal care, including hepatitis screening. Gradually, more women started showing up for proper checkups, and the maternity Centre became a beacon of hope and life. That experience solidified my passion for maternal health. Finally, I was starting to find purpose in this place.

Here is what every woman should know about hepatitis during pregnancy Hepatitis A viral infection that affects the liver and results in inflammation.  There are 5 types of hepatitis: A, B, C, D and E.  Hepatitis B and C are the deadliest forms of Hepatitis, leading to chronic diseases which, if not properly managed, can lead to liver failure or liver cancers.  How is Hepatitis B transmitted? Hepatitis can be transmitted from person to person by direct blood contact, unprotected sex, sharing hygienic equipment like a razor, toothbrush or earrings, sharing needles, tattoos, piercings, barbers or during circumcisions and from a mother to her child during birth. Hepatitis in pregnancy Viral hepatitis in pregnancy increases the risk for pregnancy‐related complications, as well as the risks associated with acute and chronic liver disease for both the mother and the infant as a result of mother‐to‐child transmission (MTCT). When a woman is pregnant, undetected hepatitis increases the risk of transmitting the virus to her baby, either during pregnancy, childbirth, or breastfeeding (in the case of Hepatitis B). Hepatitis in pregnancy can result in preterm birth (premature baby), abruption of the placenta, low birth weight, fetal distress, still birth, acute liver failure, and in the mother, hypertension in pregnancy can be observed as reported also in the community where I served.  Signs and symptoms to look out for At the early stage of hepatitis infection, there are usually no signs. Absolutely nothing tells you something is wrong there, but as the virus continues to infiltrate the liver, you begin to notice jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes), dark urine, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain and extreme fatigue. While these symptoms can also indicate other disease states, screening for hepatitis is the most effective way for you to know you have hepatitis.

Preventing Hepatitis

  • Get screened to know your status as early detection saves.
  • Vaccination provides over 95% protection against Hepatitis A and B.
  • Proper hygiene like, washing hands after using the toilet and eating properly washed and cooked food can help prevent infection with Hepatitis A and E.
  • Practice safe sex; using a condom.
  • Avoid sharing personal items and sharp objects.

Stay tuned for part 2. There you will see practical steps to keeping your baby safe.

title: Important Information Regarding the Cholera Outbreak in Ogun State
seoTitle: Important Information Regarding the Cholera Outbreak in Ogun State
html:
There is currently a cholera outbreak in the Ijebu North Local Government Area of Ogun State. Information regarding this outbreak was conveyed on Sunday, September 17th, to encourage people to implement preventive measures.

Cholera is a sudden-onset diarrheal illness resulting from consuming food or water contaminated with the Vibrio cholerae bacterium. It continues to pose a worldwide public health risk and serves as an indicator of social inequality and insufficient social progress.

Scientists have approximated that annually there are between 1.3 to 4.0 million cholera cases and 21,000 to 143,000 cholera-related deaths occurring globally.  “There is a Cholera outbreak (known as Aarun òní gbá méjì) in the Ijebu North Local Government area of Ogun State. Cholera typically occurs during rainy seasons and can be linked to inadequate environmental conditions and personal hygiene. Its symptoms often include diarrhea, sometimes accompanied by vomiting, which can lead to severe dehydration. Cholera can be fatal if prompt treatment to correct dehydration is not administered," stated the Special Adviser to the governor on Health and commissioner-designate, Tomi Coker.

The effective control of cholera and the reduction of fatalities require a comprehensive approach that encompasses various strategies. This includes surveillance, improvements in water and sanitation, hygiene promotion, social mobilization, treatment, and the utilization of oral cholera vaccines.

title: Cause for Alarm: The Resurgence of Measles, Mumps, and Rubella Diseases (MMR)
seoTitle: Cause for Alarm: The Resurgence of Measles, Mumps, and Rubella Diseases (MMR)
html:
This week, we take a deep dive into the most asked-about diseases from last week’s edition - MMR.

The MMR disease, also known as Measles, Mumps, and Rubella, is a combined term encompassing three distinct viral infections that are highly contagious, each with its own symptoms and severe complications that can lead to death.

According to the World Health Organization (WHO) statistics report, between 2000 and 2021, measles vaccination played a pivotal role in preserving the lives of 56 million individuals. However, despite the existence of a safe and affordable vaccine, 2021 witnessed a distressing figure of approximately 128,000 measles-related fatalities worldwide, predominantly among children under the age of 5 who were either unvaccinated or insufficiently vaccinated. In 2022, only 83% of children worldwide received their initial measles vaccine dose by the age of one, marking the lowest vaccination rate since 2008.

In The News: Is MMR trying to make a comeback? In London, parents are being issued a 21-day isolation notice if their children’s measles vaccinations are not up-to-date. The UK Health Security Agency has raised concerns about the potential for tens of thousands of cases, and is urging more people to get the MMR vaccine to prevent a major outbreak.

You need not worry because the MMR vaccination can provide protection for both you and your child.

“Measles is an extremely contagious disease caused by a virus that can result in complications such as brain injury, pneumonia, and even death. Symptoms include fever, rash, runny nose, cough, and red or watery eyes. The virus spreads through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes and can remain active in the air for up to 2 hours, making it highly infectious. Mumps, also caused by a virus, leads to symptoms like fever, headache, tiredness, and loss of appetite. Swollen cheeks or a swollen jawline, known as parotitis, is a characteristic sign of the disease. It also spreads through respiratory droplets from an infected person and can result in complications such as testicular inflammation in males and ovarian inflammation in females, which, although rare, can be serious. Rubella, often called German measles causes symptoms like fever, a sore throat, rash, and itchy eyes. It primarily affects children, and they usually recover quickly with no lasting effects. However, it poses a significant risk to pregnant women if they become infected, potentially causing birth defects like deafness, blindness, heart defects, or intellectual disabilities. The most effective way to prevent these diseases is through vaccination. The MMR vaccine is both safe and efficient in preventing Measles, Mumps, and Rubella, along with their associated complications. This vaccine has substantially lowered the occurrence of these diseases, underscoring the crucial role of immunization in safeguarding public health.” Get vaccinated against MMR disease today! title: Empowering Parents to Safeguard Their Kids Against School-Related Infections

A new school year is upon us. We hope you are ready for an exciting one.

Schools can be fun for children but you have to ensure that your child’s health and safety are secured especially when interacting with other kids.

Today, we will look at possible diseases or viruses that children can encounter and the importance of keeping them safe.

Did you know? “Chickenpox is caused by a virus that spreads through respiratory droplets and direct contact with fluids from chickenpox blisters of an infected person, leading to itchy rash and fever. The spots that remains after recovery can affect the beauty of your child skin, as it remains for longer periods. Also; Measles, Mumps, and Rubella (MMR) are contagious viral diseases that spread through contact with respiratory and oral droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. If your child makes contact while playing with an infected child or virally contaminated toys or objects, risk of getting infected is high. Measles infection can lead to brain damage, affecting your child’s intelligence. Long-term consequences of mumps infection include; infertility, deafness, and brain damage. Whereas, Rubella causes hearing loss, blood clotting challenges, and brain injury. Other diseases are; Flu, which causes cough, fever, or sore throat and leads to other uncomfortable complications. Common cold, that causes runny nose, cough, or mild fever and is usually not serious as it goes away on its own but can cause your child to have missed school days. Scabies, scabies is a parasitic infestation of the skin that causes severe itching and discomfort. It spreads quickly among children in close quarters. Waterborne disease, contracted from contaminated food and water, causing diseases like cholera and diarrhea. These diseases can be prevented through vaccinations, ensuring your child’s vaccinations are up-to-date, and treated in cases like scabies and waterborne diseases using anti-parasitic or anti-biotics medications, respectively. However, Practicing hand hygiene, respiratory etiquette, and a healthy lifestyle can curb the disease spread and improve the overall immunity of the community”. title: Genital Human Papillomavirus (HPV) Common in Men

A recent the Lancet Global Health publication reveals 1 in 3 sexually active men over the age of 15 are infected with at least one Human Papillomavirus (HPV) strain. In addition, the study shows around 1 in 5 men are infected with one or more cancer causing high risk HPV strains.

This means men serve as reservoir for the virus, increasing the risk of their partners getting infected. If your male partner is a carrier of the virus, your chances of getting the viral infection is high.

Furthermore, the health implications for you is that as you take steps to vaccinate yourself and your female child, also immunize your husband and boys. This ensures the immune system of a man is empowered with antibodies to clear the HPV infection. Thus, women won't easily get infected.

The Human Papillomavirus (HPV) is responsible for cervical cancer, throat and neck cancer, cancer of the penis, anal cancer, genital warts, just to mention a few.

However, there are effective vaccines that are available to enable you prevent infection by both the low and high risk HPV strains.

title: Stay Ahead of Respiratory Diseases
seoTitle: Stay Ahead of Respiratory Diseases

Hello there,

Respiratory disease can be taxing to deal with. Would you want to know a bit about it?

It is any illness that affects the airways and lungs, impacting human respiration. Around 545 million people worldwide suffer from chronic respiratory disease, ranging from common colds to conditions like asthma, pneumonia, and influenza.

These diseases affect breathing, causing discomfort and reduced quality of life. Fortunately, vaccines, like for influenza, pneumonia, and COVID-19, helps prevent respiratory infections, reducing risks.

To breathe a sigh of relief, remain aware of these vital precautions.

TODAY’s FACT: Flu Unveiled!

“Influenza, or ‘flu’, is a contagious upper respiratory tract disease caused by the influenza viruses affecting the nose, throat, and lungs. It ranges from mild to severe, and potentially fatal. The virus spreads through coughing, sneezing, or talking, releasing infected droplets into the air. It can also be transmitted via contaminated surfaces and give symptoms like fever, headaches, sore-throat, body aches, and fatigue. Vaccination is the most effective way in flu prevention, especially for the vulnerable-elderly, pregnant women, children under-five years, and those with chronic illnesses. The annual flu shot bolsters immunity against expected strains, easing symptom confusion with COVID-19 due to shared symptoms. Maintain hygiene by covering of nose and mouth when coughing or sneezing and wash hands regularly to curb virus spread”. Want to be free from potential breathing difficulties?

title: WHY PAXLOVID REMAINS YOUR GO-TO MEDICINE AGAINST COVID
seoTitle: WHY PAXLOVID REMAINS YOUR GO-TO MEDICINE AGAINST COVID
html:
Paxlovid the antiviral medication developed by Pfizer remains a very important tool in our fight against COVID-19. This medication is important in saving the lives of patients for several reasons.

First, Paxlovid is an effective oral medication against COVID. This means it can easily be self administered by patients if guided by knowledgeable healthcare professionals on the right use of the medication. The medication has shown to reduce hospitalization and death by 89.1% and 88.9%, respectively.

Secondly, the medicine has a short duration of treatment. A five days course has proven to be effective in preventing the virus from replicating. This reduces viral load and prevents complications due to the corona virus infection.

In addition, it can be taken by almost everyone and is readily accessible. The most serious drug-drug interactions associated with the medication use are interactions with rivaroxaban and salmeterol, respectively.

Lastly, it might just be a vital tool in managing Long COVID. Some experts assume Long COVID might be as a result of persistence of the virus in the body of its host. This means if you stop the virus from replicating, you're going to get less amount of viral particles in the body of the patient.

For more information on how to use and access Paxlovid contact here.

title: HOW YOU SHOULD TAKE PAXLOVID MEDICATION
seoTitle: HOW YOU SHOULD TAKE PAXLOVID MEDICATION
html:
Introduction

Paxlovid is an oral antiviral medication developed by Pfizer for the treatment of corona virus disease.

To get the benefits of the medication you will have to take it for the recommended period.

How is Paxlovid packaged

This simply is how the medication is packed in its pack and sachet.

One sachet of the antiviral medication contains nirmatrelvir 300 mg (two 150 mg tablets) co-packaged with 100 mg ritonavir tablet.

How do I take Paxlovid

The dosing recommendation or dose regimen is how you take the medication. This specifies the quantity of the medication you should take, the number of times in a day you should take it, and for how long. This determines whether you will get the benefits of the medication.

You're advised to take two tablets of nirmatrelvir and one tablet of ritonavir. All three tablets should be taken together twice daily (every 12 hours) for a period of five days. This means you should take your first dose either in the morning or evening. This depends on the time your healthcare professional recommends.

The manufacturers recommend you consider the following when taking Paxlovid:

  • Do not remove your PAXLOVID tablets from the blister card before you are ready to take your dose
  • All tablets in a dose should be taken at once or one right after the other
  • Swallow tablets whole. Do not break, chew, or crush the tablets
  • PAXLOVID can be taken with or without food

For further information on how to get Paxlovid please contact us on 08160962414 or visit here for assistance.

You can read our previous article on three things you should know about Paxlovid here.

title: WHAT YOU SHOULD KNOW ABOUT PAXLOVID AND LONG COVID
seoTitle: WHAT YOU SHOULD KNOW ABOUT PAXLOVID AND LONG COVID
html:
Research findings says people who took Paxlovid are less likely to have long COVID symptoms. The article was published in March 2023 in JAMA Internal Medicine. It revealed that high-risk patients who took Paxlovid within five days of confirming infection for COVID-19 were 26% less likely to have Long COVID symptoms after 90 days, compared to people who weren’t treated.  Long COVID are myriad of symptoms experienced by people after recovering from COVID-19. The study followed through on more than 35,000 patients who took Paxlovid for a six month period. This research was of great importance as the medical community debates what causes Long COVID, and contributes to the understanding of the symptoms and possible causes.

Dr. Ziyad Al-Aly, a clinical epidemiologist at the Washington University School of Medicine in St. Louis and co-author of the JAMA study on Paxlovid, said: '' Despite the unknowns, certain patients may want to pursue Paxlovid prescriptions. For people at high risk of severe COVID-19, who already stand to benefit from Paxlovid, the possibility of preventing Long COVID is an added bonus.'' title: WHAT YOU SHOULD KNOW ABOUT PAXLOVID APPROVAL
seoTitle: WHAT YOU SHOULD KNOW ABOUT PAXLOVID APPROVAL
html:
Jennifer Lorenzini/Reuters The U.S Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approves Paxlovid for the treatment of mild-to-moderate COVID-19. In a press release On May 25, 2023, the FDA granted Pfizer approval for its antiviral medication for the treatment of mild-to-moderate COVID-19 in adults who are at high risk for progression to severe COVID-19, including hospitalization or death. The decision means the medication met regulatory safety and efficacy standards, and allows the Pfizer’s drug to remain on the market indefinitely.

Paxlovid is an important medication for the treatment of mild-to-moderate coronavirus in adults older than 50 and people with certain underlying medical conditions like diabetes, heart conditions, cancer or a weak immune system, amongst others. The data showed that Paxlovid significantly reduced the proportion of people with COVID-19 related hospitalization or death from any cause through 28 days of follow-up by 86% compared to placebo among patients treated within five days of symptom onset and who did not receive COVID-19 therapeutic monoclonal antibody treatment. In this analysis, 977 patients received Paxlovid, and 989 patients received placebo, and among these patients, 0.9% who received Paxlovid were hospitalized due to COVID-19 or died from any cause during 28 days of follow-up compared to 6.5% of the patients who received the placebo.

Dr. Patrizia Cavazzoni, director for the FDA’s Center for Drug Evaluation and Research, said: ''Today’s approval demonstrates that Paxlovid has met the agency’s rigorous standards for safety and effectiveness, and that it remains an important treatment option for people at high risk for progression to severe COVID-19, including those with prior immunity.'' title: PAXLOVID: LOWER RISK OF SEVERE COVID IN PATIENTS WITH UNDERLYING CHRONIC CONDITIONS
seoTitle: PAXLOVID: LOWER RISK OF SEVERE COVID IN PATIENTS WITH UNDERLYING CHRONIC CONDITIONS
html:
Paxlovid reduces risk of severe COVID-19 in patients with chronic health condition a new study finds. The study which was conducted by researchers at Harvard University was published in June 2023 in the Clinical Infectious Diseases Journal. This investigation will contribute to proper use of the antiviral medication in the treatment of patients in the most cost effective manner.

This study is important because as governments funding for COVID-19 treatment reduces, cost of the medication is likely to increase. This means Clinicians need correct information on cost effective means to treat COVID-19, prescribing antiviral medications for those who need them the most. The study was needed because the effect of Paxlovid on COVID-19 outcomes in younger vaccinated adults are unclear.

Researchers in the study assessed outcomes among two groups of 2,547 COVID-19 patients aged 18 to 50 years. Paxlovid was linked to a 30% lower risk of all-cause emergency department visits, hospitalization, and death among vaccinated, non-hospitalized COVID-19 patients with serious chronic conditions. In addition, Paxlovid recipients had lower rates of constitutional, cardiovascular, and respiratory symptoms than controls.

title: 3 THINGS YOU SHOULD KNOW ABOUT PAXLOVID
seoTitle: 3 THINGS YOU SHOULD KNOW ABOUT PAXLOVID
html:
Paxlovid is an oral antiviral medication for the treatment of COVID-19. It was developed by Pfizer for the treatment of mild-to-moderate COVID-19 in adults at high risk for severe disease, including hospitalization and death. The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) first made Paxlovid available in December 2021 under emergency use authorization for high-risk individuals ages 12 and above. Paxlovid is a very important drug to save the lives of people infected by COVID-19. Here are three things you should know about Paxlovid.

When should you take Paxlovid ?

You should take the medication within five days of developing COVID-19 symptoms. This is because once you've been sick due to the virus for more than a week, the medications won't be able to restore the damages the virus must have caused to your body.

How long should you take Paxlovid ?

The medication should be taken for a duration of five days. Your healthcare provider will recommend you take three Paxlovid pills every 12 hours for five days to get the full benefit of the medication.

Where can you get Paxlovid ?

You should be able to get the medication from your hospital or neighborhood pharmacy. However, due to scarcity of the medication only few healthcare facilities might have it available at the time when it is urgently needed.

title: HPV and throat and neck cancer: What you should know
seoTitle: HPV and throat and neck cancer: What you should know
html:
The oral Human papillomavirus (HPV) can be gotten through oral sex. But what are the implications of oral HPV infection? Are there ways to prevent it? These are the key questions we will be answering on this article.

HPV is a very contagious virus, with over 100 strains that causes numerous diseases such as genital warts, cervical cancer, and other cancers such as cancers of the anus, vagina, vulva, and penis.

It will be very important to note that HPV is responsible for over 70% of throat and neck cancer, what medically is called ‘’oropharyngeal cancer. ‘’ However, other factors such as smoking or chewing tobacco, alcohol use, and gastroesophageal reflux disease might be contributing factors.

In addition, after infection this cancer takes years to develop and it is a very difficult cancer to screen for, so unlike cervical cancer which can be detected on time by doing a pap smear or HPV test, there isn’t a known test to detect throat cancer in a timely fashion, but physical assessment by your doctor or dentist during a regular visit for check-up especially when you are experiencing symptoms such as chronic cough, ear pain, difficulties when swallowing, prolonged sore throat, throat pain, voice changes, lumps or swelling in the throat or lymph nodes, and unexplained weight loss might be very helpful. More so, some persons might not experience any symptom.

How do we prevent throat infection by HPV? It is the dream of everyone to lead a happy life and be here for our loved ones, friends, and families. Preventing the suffering and pains associated with infectious diseases is important in achieving our dreams. There several ways to prevent HPV infection of the mouth and they include: quit smoking, stop or reduce intake of alcohol, make use of Dental Dams, but one of the most effective way is to get vaccinated against HPV; there are about three major vaccines but the human papillomavirus 9-valent vaccine is the most effective means.

title: Hepatitis B: Have no cure but you should know this!
seoTitle: Hepatitis B: Have no cure but you should know this!
html:
Most times when people test positive for viral hepatitis B; they begin to wonder how they got it? So many questions start to run through their mind. In this article, we are going to discuss mother-to-child transmission of hepatitis B virus infection , how it might be a cause of hepatitis B in people who don’t know how they got the virus, and how it can be prevented.

When Dayo was first told by his Doctor that he was hepatitis B positive, the first questions from him was '' what is hepatitis B? how did I get it? '' he asked.

''Hepatitis B is an infection caused by the hepatitis B virus (HBV) transmitted via infected blood and body fluid'' he was told.

But how did he get it? There are several ways a person can contract hepatitis B virus infection, but mother-to-child transmission is one of the most common routes.

When a woman is pregnant, at her first prenatal (during pregnancy) visit to the clinic, she is screened for viral hepatitis B – this ensures early detection.

Why is this important? It is estimated that 90% of children who contract hepatitis B from their mother during the process of childbirth will go on to live with it for the rest of their lives.

Furthermore, the risk of mother-to-child transmission is significantly higher in expectant mothers who have plasma HBV DNA levels greater than 2,000 IU/ML and/or are hepatitis B envelope antigen-positive (HBeAg positive).

In the absence of antiviral therapy and immunoprophylaxis (hepatitis B vaccine and hepatitis B immunoglobulin) about 70% to 90% of babies born to hepatitis B envelope positive (HBeAg positive), mothers might become positive for hepatitis B.

What can a family do to prevent their infant from getting infected with hepatitis B, if a mother is hepatitis B positive? First, ensure an expectant mother get screened for hepatitis B and knows her status before term.

Secondly, antiviral therapy to prevent mother-to-child transmission should be initiated according to experts during week 28 to 32 of gestation, if a mother HBV DNA level is greater than 200,000 IU/mL and it should be discontinued at delivery, however, there are other factors to be considered.

Additionally, positive women who don't meet the above criteria should monitor their status by doing a liver function test every 3 months during pregnancy and every 6 months after giving birth – the level of Alanine aminotransferase enzyme (ALT) has a lot to reveal.

Lastly, the baby should receive birth dose hepatitis B vaccination and hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG) within 12 hours of birth: this reduces the risk of a baby contracting hepatitis B by at least 92%.

In conclusion, chances are high Dayo must have gotten hepatitis B as a child from his infected mother, his condition was asymptomatic, and he got to discover his status 20 to 30 years later.
